start.text: Midnight. The chapel cellar. Maren sits at his desk surrounded by drafts — crumpled parchment littering the floor like shed skin. A single candle throws his shadow long against the wall. He is composing another false report, and his hands are shaking.

writing_scene.speaker: brother_maren
writing_scene.text: I write that the poisoning continues. That the village weakens. That Phase 2 should be delayed for maximum effect. I write that the herbalist suspects nothing. That the old woman is frail and failing. That the lord drinks himself to sleep each night and will not resist.

writing_2.speaker: brother_maren
writing_2.text: Every lie I write buys you another day. But the lies are getting harder to sustain. Sister Vesper is not stupid — she'll notice the inconsistencies eventually. The reports describe a village that should be collapsing by now. When they send someone to verify...

hands_shaking.text: He pauses. Looks at his hands — the tremor is visible. He presses them flat against the desk to still them. Silas's hands. Old Maren's hands. The hands that measured nightcap root into the well for months. The hands that stopped.

options.text: How much longer can you keep this up?
Tell me more about the counter-rite. We need to be ready when the time comes.
About Old Maren. I need to talk to you about the promise I made.
What you're doing matters. I want you to know that.

timeline.speaker: brother_maren
timeline.text: Two weeks. Maybe less. The Church expects results on a schedule — the nightcap root should have weakened the village enough for Phase 2 by now. I've been attributing the delay to 'forest interference' — claiming the Thornwood is metabolizing the poison, requiring higher doses. It's plausible enough to buy time.

timeline_2.speaker: brother_maren
timeline_2.text: But Sister Vesper will send an inspector. She always does. When that inspector arrives and sees a village that is recovering rather than dying, the army follows within days. We have until the inspector comes. After that, the false reports become a death sentence — for me, and for everyone I've been trying to protect.

rite_details.speaker: brother_maren
rite_details.text: The counter-rite is a truth-speaking ceremony performed at the Heartwood — the oldest tree in Thornwood, the nexus of the pact. Each participant must speak their deepest truth. Not a confession extracted under duress. Not a recitation of facts. The truth that defines them. The secret that shapes their soul.

rite_details_2.speaker: brother_maren
rite_details_2.text: The pact feeds on lies — on the gap between what people are and what they pretend to be. The wider the gap, the stronger the pact's hold. Truth-speaking closes the gap. Enough truths spoken freely at the Heartwood, and the pact has nothing left to feed on. It dissolves.

rite_details_3.speaker: brother_maren
rite_details_3.text: The key word is 'freely.' Coerced truth has no weight. Extracted confessions feed the pact rather than starving it — they add another layer of violation. Each speaker must choose to speak. Must want to be known. It is the hardest thing you can ask of a person, and we need to ask it of everyone who matters in Ashvale.

rite_details_4.speaker: brother_maren
rite_details_4.text: My truth: I poisoned this village because I was afraid of what killed my father. I turned my grief into a weapon and aimed it at the people my father died to protect. That truth, spoken freely at the Heartwood, would carry enormous weight. The poisoner himself, confessing at the nexus of the pact he was sent to reinforce.

promise_conversation.speaker: brother_maren
promise_conversation.text: The promise. You're going to tell me you need to break it. That the counter-rite needs every truth, including the one I asked you to bury. That Old Maren's truth — 'I recognized my grandson and said nothing' — could be the weight that tips the ceremony.

options.text: Yes. I need to tell her. The rite needs her truth, and she can't speak it if she doesn't know you know.
No. I made a promise. I'll find another way to power the rite.
It should be your choice. YOU tell her. Or don't. But the rite is stronger with her truth in it.

promise_broken_reaction.speaker: brother_maren
promise_broken_reaction.text: ...I knew. The moment I asked you to keep that secret, I knew you wouldn't. Not because you're cruel — because you're practical. And the practical thing is to use every weapon available. Even an old woman's grief.

promise_broken_2.speaker: brother_maren
promise_broken_2.text: Then let me be there when you tell her. Don't let her hear it from a stranger. Let her look at my face when she learns what her grandson did. She deserves that much. And so do I.

promise_kept_reaction.speaker: brother_maren
promise_kept_reaction.text: Thank you. I know what it costs you — a weaker rite, a harder path. But some things matter more than strategy. She will die not knowing. It is a kindness wrapped in a cruelty, or perhaps the other way around. I can no longer tell the difference.

maren_decides.speaker: brother_maren
maren_decides.text: My choice. You're asking me to choose between my grandmother's peace and Ashvale's survival. Between one old woman's heart and an entire village.

maren_decides_2.speaker: brother_maren
maren_decides_2.text: ...I'll tell her. Myself. Tomorrow morning. She takes her tea on the bench by the village edge at dawn. I'll sit with her and I'll tell her everything. If the rite needs her truth, then she needs to know the truth first. And it should come from me. Not from you. Not from a document. From her grandson's mouth.

gratitude_response.speaker: brother_maren
gratitude_response.text: Don't. Don't thank the man who poisoned your village for writing lies that might delay the army coming to burn it. That is not heroism. That is the barest minimum of decency from a man who spent months below it.

gratitude_2.speaker: brother_maren
gratitude_2.text: But... I hear you. And it has been a long time since anyone told me that anything I did mattered. The Church told me I mattered — as a weapon. You're the first person who told me I mattered as a man. I don't know what to do with that. So I'll do the only thing I know how: I'll write another lie that buys another day.
